title: AMD And Nvidia Are Neck And Neck In HPC Supercomputing
gist: China's new 'LineShine' supercomputer, based on a homegrown Armv9-compatible CPU co-designed with Huawei's HiSilicon, has reclaimed the global #1 supercomputing rank at 2.2 exaflops HPL, surpassing AMD's El Capitan by 21.5%. In the broader June 2026 Top500 list, AMD and Nvidia are nearly tied in accelerated HPC capacity — AMD at 8.18 exaflops versus Nvidia at 11 exaflops — suggesting real competitive parity that could foreshadow the AI compute market.

themes.theme: China Regains HPC Supremacy with Indigenous Silicon

insights.claim: China's 'LineShine' supercomputer at NSC Shenzhen is now the world's fastest, delivering 2.2 exaflops on HPL — 21.5% more than the previous leader, AMD-based El Capitan at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The U.S. no longer holds the top supercomputing position; China's HPC lead has direct implications for scientific and defense R&D competition.
insights.quote: LineShine delivers just a tad under 2.2 exaflops of oomph and that makes it 21.5 percent more powerful than the former top ranked machine, the 'El Capitan' supercomputer based on AMD MI300A compute engines located at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory.

insights.claim: LineShine is built on the LingKun LX2 CPU with 304 active cores per chip, co-designed by NSC Shenzhen and Huawei (HiSilicon), using an Armv9-compatible architecture with SVE2 vector and SME matrix units.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: China has demonstrated a credible, indigenous high-performance CPU design outside U.S. supply chains, reducing leverage of export controls.
insights.quote: The LingKun LX2 CPU design has 304 active cores, and very likely there are more cores on the chip to increase the yield.

insights.claim: LineShine uses a proprietary LingQi LQLink interconnect that is likely derived from InfiniBand or enhanced Ethernet technology.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: China is also indigenizing high-speed interconnects — a historically U.S./Mellanox-dominated supply chain — reducing another point of dependency.
insights.quote: The LineShine machine has a proprietary LingQi LQLink interconnect, which I am reasonably sure is based on a variation of InfiniBand technology but it could be a jacked-up and stripped-down version of Ethernet.

insights.claim: China likely has several more undisclosed exascale machines beyond the three publicly known systems (LineShine at 2.2 exaflops, OceanLight at 1.5 exaflops, Tianhe-3 at 2.05 exaflops).
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: China's true HPC capacity is likely significantly larger than official Top500 figures suggest, amplifying the strategic capability gap.
insights.quote: We strongly suspect that the country has several more exascale machines that it is not talking about much.

insights.claim: China's strategy of building CPU-only, MPI-based supercomputers without GPU offload allows rapid scaling using indigenous chips while avoiding the complexity of accelerator ecosystems.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: This architectural approach is a viable path to exascale sovereignty and sidesteps U.S. GPU export controls entirely.
insights.quote: Necessity is always the mother of invention, and if you are trying to create an indigenous compute engine design and manufacturing capability, as China certainly is... create all-CPU machines that do not have an offload model and that simply use MPI to spread calculations across traditional scale out networks.

themes.theme: AMD vs Nvidia: Near-Parity in HPC Accelerator Market

insights.claim: In the June 2026 Top500, AMD has 8.18 exaflops of installed HPC accelerated capacity versus Nvidia's 11 exaflops, with AMD at 32 systems to Nvidia's 237 systems — AMD achieves comparable aggregate flops with far fewer but denser machines.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: AMD's per-machine efficiency advantage in HPC accelerators is substantial; this metric is a forward signal for AMD's competitiveness in the AI accelerator market.
insights.quote: AMD has 8.18 exaflops of installed capacity compared to Nvdia's 11 exaflops, and in terms of concurrency, AMD has sold accelerated machines with a combine CPU and GPU concurrency of 35.3 million cores compared to Nvidia's 38.9 million cores. This is a real race, and perhaps portends the future of AI computing as well.

insights.claim: Many HPC centers deploying Nvidia GPUs are choosing older 'Hopper' H100/H200 GPUs over the newer Blackwell B200/B300, citing better FP64 performance per dollar.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Blackwell's FP64 regression relative to Hopper is a real purchasing friction in scientific HPC; Nvidia's AI-optimized roadmap may create an opening for AMD in traditional HPC.
insights.quote: Hopper GPUs are cheaper, and they also have more FP64 flops and more flops per dollar than the follow-on 'Blackwell' B200 and B300 GPUs.

insights.claim: Eni's HPC7 system, based on AMD MI300A and ranked #6 globally, is the largest commercial supercomputer with publicly submitted Top500 results.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: AMD's MI300A architecture is winning large commercial HPC contracts in energy — a sector that validates workload-specific value beyond AI hype.
insights.quote: The other big new machine is the HPC7 system at Italian oil and gas giant Eni, which is based on AMD's hybrid CPU-GPU MI300A accelerator; HPC7 is essentially a chip off the El Capitan block, and is ranked number six on the list.

themes.theme: HPC Investment Slowdown and Moore's Law Divergence

insights.claim: HPC supercomputing capacity growth has fallen off Moore's Law's historical two-year doubling cadence, and current spending levels are insufficient to maintain that pace.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Sovereign HPC spending outside China is under-indexed relative to scientific and strategic needs; this is a policy and budget gap, not a technology gap.
insights.quote: We have still fallen off the Moore's Law wagon of doubling performance every two years, and at least in supercomputing, we are not spending enough money to get passage on the wagon.

insights.claim: The June 2026 Top500 added 44 new machines contributing approximately 5.3 exaflops of aggregate new capacity, with LineShine alone accounting for 51.6% of that addition.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: A single Chinese machine dominated half of all new global HPC capacity additions in this cycle, concentrating the leading edge in one jurisdiction.
insights.quote: There are 44 new machines this time around... LineShine machine, which comprises 51.6 percent of the aggregate new 5.3 exaflops capacity added to the June list.

insights.claim: The minimum HPL benchmark score to appear on the June 2026 Top500 list is 2.66 petaflops, a threshold easily achievable by modern CPUs or GPUs, reflecting rising baseline compute density.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The Top500 floor is rising rapidly; organizations not investing in hardware refreshes are being pushed off the list, compressing the competitive field.
insights.quote: You have to have 2.66 petaflops of HPL bang to even get on the list this time around. Which frankly is not that much given how much performance you can get out of a modern CPU or GPU.

themes.theme: CPU Architecture Diversity Persists in HPC

insights.claim: Intel Xeon paired with Nvidia GPUs remains the most common hybrid HPC configuration, with 11 such new machines on the June 2026 list, followed by 9 machines pairing AMD Epyc with Nvidia GPUs.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Intel retains significant HPC CPU market share despite AMD's momentum; Nvidia GPU adoption spans both CPU camps, reinforcing its ecosystem stickiness.
insights.quote: There were eleven such new machines on the June list, and there were another nine machines that had AMD Epyc CPUs paired with Nvidia GPUs.

insights.claim: CPU-only HPC clusters (no accelerators) continue to be deployed, with five new AMD Epyc-only and four new Intel Xeon-only machines on the June 2026 list.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: A non-trivial segment of HPC workloads remains best served by CPU-only architectures, sustaining demand for high-core-count server CPUs.
insights.quote: CPU-only machines are not taking over the world, but they are not going away.
